data.title: Moderate U.S. Inflation Supports Fed's Summer Pause — Market Talk
data.description: A weaker-than-expected U.S. CPI print leads Julius Baer to reaffirm its base case for the Federal Reserve to hold rates over the summer before cutting later in the year amid a slowing economy. Economist Julian Schaerer expects two 50bp rate cuts in September and October, anticipating that economic momentum will weaken more visibly in 2H. "While tariffs could exert some upward pressure on prices later this year, we view inflation risks as increasingly balanced," Schaerer says in a note. The Fed is likely to respond decisively once macroeconomic indicators, particularly those related to growth and the labor market, begin to turn. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

data.title: Lifecare ASA: Notification of trade - exercise of warrants
data.description:
Reference is made to the stock exchange announcement by Lifecare ASA (the "Company") dated 2 June 2025 regarding the commencement of the exercise period for 4,193,806 warrants (the "Warrants") issued in connection with the partially underwritten rights issue of new shares in the Company completed in June 2024 (the "Rights Issue").

Each Warrant gives the holder a right to subscribe for one new share in the Company at an exercise price per share of NOK 5.31681. Warrants that are not exercised to subscribe for new shares before the end of the Exercise Period at 16:30 hours (CEST) on 13 June 2025 will have no value and will lapse without compensation to the holder. Holders of Warrants who do not exercise their Warrants within the Exercise Period may experience a dilution of their shareholding in the Company.

The Company has been informed that Hannibal Invest AS, a company closely related to Hans Hekland, Board member and primary insider of Lifecare ASA, has exercised 1,335 Warrants for the allocation of 1,335 New Shares in the Company at a price per New Share of NOK 5.31681.

Subject to timely payment of the aggregate exercise amount for the new shares on 19 June 2025, the Company expects that the share capital increase pertaining to the issuance of the new shares will be registered with the Norwegian Register of Business Enterprises on or about 24 June 2025 and that shares will be delivered to the VPS accounts of the subscribers to whom they are allocated on or about 25 June 2025.

The shares received upon exercise of Warrants will be listed on Euronext Oslo Børs under ISIN NO0013355859 and ticker code "LIFE". The shares will be listed as soon as the new shares are fully paid, the share capital increase pertaining to the issuance of the new shares has been registered in the Norwegian Register of Business Enterprises, and the new shares have been issued in the VPS.

The shares received upon exercise of Warrants may not be transferred or traded before (i) the new shares have been fully paid, (ii) the share capital increase pertaining to the issuance of the new shares has been registered with the Norwegian Register of Business Enterprises, and (iii) the new shares have been issued in the VPS.

data.title: Halma's Guidance Looks Very Strong — Market Talk
data.description: Halma's guidance for fiscal 2026 looks very strong on organic growth, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. The U.K. engineering group is guiding for upper single-digit organic growth but a higher foreign-exchange rate headwind is likely to leave consensus forecasts broadly unchanged, the analysts say. The beat and the organic guidance supported by further growth in photonics should drives shares on Thursday, they say. Shares trade 7.6% higher at 32.50 pounds, leading the FTSE 100 index risers. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

data.title: Top Wall Street bank pitches options play to ride potential Indian rupee rally to 83
data.description:
Goldman Sachs' sales and trading desk is recommending an options play on the Indian rupee that will pay off if the South Asian currency rallies to 83 per U.S. dollar over the next nine months.

The call — effectively a bet on a 3% appreciation in the rupee from its current level of 85.50 — is underpinned by India's improving macroeconomic fundamentals, a revival in foreign inflows, lower oil prices, and the potential for a U.S.-India trade deal.

The rupee has lagged behind its Asian peers this year, showing little response to the dollar index’s more than 9% decline.

Goldman Sachs is recommending buying a 9-month USD/INR binary put option with a strike price of 83. A binary put option is a type of digital option that pays a fixed amount if the currency pair settles below the strike level at the expiry of the contract.

"We chose 9-month tenor for the trade as INR typically tends to appreciate during India’s financial year end", which concludes on March 31, according to a sales note from Goldman Sachs.

In support of their constructive outlook on the rupee, Goldman Sachs analysts highlighted that India’s GDP growth accelerated to 7.4% year-on-year in the March quarter from 6.4% in the previous three months.

Their monthly activity tracker indicates that consumption remained robust in April.

The investment bank's note pointed to a return of foreign equity inflows, with over $4 billion flowing into Indian equities over the past two months. Goldman expects this trend to continue and potentially accelerate, driven by improving corporate earnings.

The possibility of a U.S.-India trade deal and lower oil prices could be other catalysts for the rupee. Goldman said that a rollback of the 10% reciprocal tariff would be seen as a positive development for Indian risk assets and the rupee.

While the U.S. had initially proposed a 26% levy on Indian shipmemts, the country-specific tariffs have been paused until July 8.

On oil, Goldman's commodities research team expects Brent crude to average $60 for the remainder of 2025 and fall to $56 in 2026. Lower energy prices are a net positive for oil-importing countries like India and could support the rupee.

data.title: UK Market Talk Roundup: Shares Gaining
data.description:
Stocks on the rise in the UK today. Compiled by Dow Jones Newswires Markets Desk, markets.eu@dowjones.com

0815 GMT - Shares in gold miners trade up as futures in the precious metal are boosted by safe-haven demand. Gold futures trade up 0.6% at $3,364.20 a troy ounce, having risen as high as $3,398.40/oz earlier in the session. Investor appetite for gold has been boosted by geopolitical uncertainty in the Middle East. In South Africa, AngloGold Ashanti rises 2.5%, Gold Fields is up 2.2% while Harmony Gold rises 1.1%. In London, Endeavour Mining rises 0.8% while gold and silver miner Fresnillo rises 0.3%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0813 GMT - Halma's guidance for fiscal 2026 looks very strong on organic growth, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. The U.K. engineering group is guiding for upper single-digit organic growth but a higher foreign-exchange rate headwind is likely to leave consensus forecasts broadly unchanged, the analysts say. The beat and the organic guidance supported by further growth in photonics should drives shares on Thursday, they say. Shares trade 7.6% higher at 32.50 pounds, leading the FTSE 100 index risers. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0800 GMT - BP could announce a flurry of disposals in the second half of the year, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. They write this after hosting a virtual call with BP's Chief Executive Murray Auchincloss. In the near term, the focus is on disposing infrastructure assets and some deals could be announced relatively soon, they say. Work on the Gelsenkirken refinery disposal continues while the Lightsource process is due to launch imminently, they add. Overall, BP is confident it can reach its $20 billion disposal target, the analysts write. Shares trade up 1.7% at 380 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0746 GMT - Crest Nicholson's new strategy is working as it posts a reassuring set of 1H results, RBC Capital Markets analysts Anthony Codling and Oliver Dyson say in a note. Its current performance is in line with views and the outlook seems more calm than stormy, the analysts say. The housebuilder is better organized and better placed to cope with changeable housing market conditions after addressing legacy performance issues and is rebuilding a profitable and sustainable business, the analysts say. "Crest is well placed to benefit from improving market conditions and as the stars align on planning and funding for social and affordable housing, Crest is on course to deliver on its medium-term guidance" the analysts say. Shares are up 0.5% at 190.20 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0734 GMT - Tesco's dominance in the British supermarket sector is undiminished, Interactive Investor's Richard Hunter writes in a note. For the first quarter of fiscal 2026, the U.K. grocer booked an increase in revenue of 4.6% on a like-for-like basis to 16.38 billion pounds. Tesco's current market share of 28% is more than that of its two nearest rivals, Sainsbury and Asda, combined, with 15% and 12%, respectively. Tesco has managed to lower prices in a highly competitive sector and against higher costs including national insurance contributions, Hunter says. Shares trade 0.7% higher at 388 pence. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0722 GMT - Tesco has managed to gain market share in recent years on a strong performance, RBC Capital Markets analysts Manjari Dhar and Richard Chamberlain say in a note. The British grocer booked 16.38 billion pounds in group sales excluding value-added tax and fuel for the 13 weeks ended May 24. Like-for-like sales rose 4.6% over the period. Tesco seems a best-in-class player in the U.K. food retail space, they say. The company has a strong business model and an experienced management team, the analysts add. However, a step-up in competitiveness by some key peers could mean that further gains are more difficult to come by, they say. Shares are up 0.6% at 387.8 pence. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

Contact us in London. +44-20-7842-9288
Markettalk.eu@dowjones.com

data.description:
EPA to Repeal Air-Pollution Standards on Power Plants

The proposals would roll back Biden-era climate rules and Obama-era mercury limits.

----

How Amazon's Nuclear Deal Could Solve a Touchy Political Problem

The data-center agreement with Talen Energy could help ease fears of rising electricity prices for homeowners.

----

U.S. Moves to Withdraw Some Middle East Personnel as Iran Tensions Rise

The State Department ordered all nonessential staff to leave the U.S. Embassy in Baghdad and authorized the departure of nonessential staff and families from Bahrain and Kuwait.

----

Oil Futures Soar on Raft of Supportive News

Oil futures extended gains to recover "Liberation Day" levels as the U.S. and China reached a tentative trade agreement.

----

U.S. Crude-Oil Stockpiles Down More Than Expected

U.S. crude-oil inventories fell last week by 3.6 million barrels, surpassing an expected decline of 1.6 million-barrels.

----

NRG Energy Used This Accounting Trick to Make Its Earnings Look Less Volatile

The company's interpretation of accounting rules allows it to mask swings in its derivative contracts.

----

Kimmeridge Cements Plan to Build Integrated Natural-Gas Company

Backing from sovereign-wealth investor Mubadala helped open the door for a U.S. wellhead-to-water business.

----

The Audacious Reboot of America's Nuclear Energy Program

AI and competition with China are pushing startups like Standard Nuclear to reinvent atomic energy.

----

EU Proposes Nord Stream Ban, Cutting Oil Price Cap to Hit Russia

The European Union's executive arm proposed banning transactions with Russia's Nord Stream gas pipelines and cutting the price cap on Russian oil as part of its latest round of sanctions against the Kremlin.

----

Ingka Group Buys Dutch Solar Farms

The acquisition adds three solar parks with a capacity of 76.3 megawatts to the IKEA store owner's portfolio of renewable assets.

----

EIA Sees Lower U.S. Crude-Oil Production as Drilling Slows

The EIA expects production to average a little above 13.4 million barrels a day this year, and just under 13.4 million barrels a day in 2026.

----

Tech Industry Fights to Save Clean-Energy Tax Credits

Microsoft and Google have been leading backers of technologies such as solar projects and battery storage.

data.title: Bitcoin Price Falls. Why Crypto Volatility Is a Thing of the Past. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Elsa Ohlen and George Glover

Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies fell early Thursday, with the world's largest digital coin stopping short of another all-time high after edging closer to a record over the past few days.

Bitcoin traded at $107,736 Thursday morning, down 1.6% over the past 24 hours, according to CoinDesk data. It's down from an intraday high of $110,332 Wednesday, just about 1% from its record high of just below $112,000 in mid May.

Among other cryptos, Ether was down 1.1%, XRP dropped 1.2% and Solana fell 4.4%, all giving up some of Wednesday's gains following softer-than-expected inflation data.

The industry is stubbornly bullish, though.

"We're not just watching Bitcoin break records — we're seeing the end of an era. Volatility once defined crypto. Now, sustained strength points to a maturing market shaped by real-world utility, regulatory progress, and global adoption," said Roshan Robert, U.S. CEO of OKX, a company that operates a crypto exchange and wallet.

Looking at history and cyclical patterns, Bitcoin should do well for about another year, then see a down year in 2026, Cantor Fitzgerald analyst Brett Knoblauch told Barron's.

However, the world of crypto is evolving. "We've never had a pro-crypto administration or this level of institutional adoption," Knoblauch, who is a managing director for crypto and digital assets research at the brokerage, said.

"Bitcoin is now that global reserve to play against monetary debasement. I'm optimistic about where it's going... I think the broader environment is going to be quite supportive for the next five to six months."

Write to Elsa Ohlen at elsa.ohlen@barrons.com and George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Norway companies expect firm growth in Q2 and Q3, central bank survey shows
data.description:
Norwegian companies expect growth to remain firm in the second and third quarters despite global trade tensions, a central bank survey showed on Thursday.

"International trade barriers are increasing uncertainty, but only a few contacts expect that this will lead to lower growth in the period to autumn," Norges Bank said in a statement accompanying the survey of businesses.

Increased defence investment and higher output from the fish farming industry are boosting the economy, and companies also expect higher sales of durable goods and further growth in tourism, it added.

Participants have revised up their annual wage growth projections for 2025 to 4.5% from the 4.4% seen in March , the quarterly survey showed, while expectations for 2026 were raised to 4.0% from 3.9%.

The central bank is due to announce its next interest rate decision on June 19.

data.title: India's Ashoka Buildcon falls on search by tax authority
data.description:
** Shares of Ashoka Buildcon fall 2.5% to 212 rupees

** Goods & Services Tax department serves notice for search and seizure at company's Nashik office; co says search began on June 11

** Stock was flat ahead of the search announcement

** ABDL set to fall for a fourth straight session, dropping 5% in the period

** YTD, stock is down 32%

data.title: Tesco CEO: Market Remains Intensely Competitive
DHL Group Profits Likely Remain Resilient — Market Talk
data.description: DHL Group's second quarter results should show that profits have held up well despite the market's volatility, Deutsche Bank analyst Andy Chu writes. The bank is positive on DHL as it has market-leading assets, largely with high barriers to entry, and a proven ability to adapt to uncertainty. It expects free cash flow at around 3 billion euros, allowing the company to pay at least a flat dividend of 1.85 euros per share and buy back shares. "We acknowledge that DHL is a cyclical stock and has volatile earnings, but we appreciate the resilient free cash flow, which we believe supports the medium-term buy case." Deutsche Bank lifts its target price to 47 euros from 42 euros. Shares fall 0.5% to 40.78 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

About us

South32 is a globally diversified mining and metals company. Our purpose is to make a difference by developing natural resources, improving people's lives now and for generations to come. We are trusted by our owners and partners to realise the potential of their resources. We produce commodities including bauxite, alumina, aluminium, copper, zinc, lead, silver, nickel and manganese from our operations in Australia, Southern Africa and South America. We also have a portfolio of high-quality development projects and options, and exploration prospects, consistent with our strategy to reshape our portfolio towards commodities critical for a low-carbon future.

data.title: Ework Group CEO/President to Step Down
data.description:
Ework Group Chief Executive and President Karin Schreil decided to step down at the end of 2025.

The Swedish supplier of IT and technology consultants said Thursday it started the recruitment process to find a new CEO.

data.title: Hang Seng Tech Index Ends 2.2% Lower at 5331.33
Hang Seng Index Ends 1.4% Lower at 24035.38
Lifecare ASA: Notification of trade - exercise of warrants
data.description:
Reference is made to the stock exchange announcement by Lifecare ASA (the "Company") dated 2 June 2025 regarding the commencement of the exercise period for 4,193,806 warrants (the "Warrants") issued in connection with the partially underwritten rights issue of new shares in the Company completed in June 2024 (the "Rights Issue").

Each Warrant gives the holder a right to subscribe for one new share in the Company at an exercise price per share of NOK 5.31681. Warrants that are not exercised to subscribe for new shares before the end of the Exercise Period at 16:30 hours (CEST) on 13 June 2025 will have no value and will lapse without compensation to the holder. Holders of Warrants who do not exercise their Warrants within the Exercise Period may experience a dilution of their shareholding in the Company.

The Company has been informed that Tone Kvåle, Board member and primary insider of Lifecare ASA, has exercised 1,539 Warrants for the allocation of 1,539 New Shares in the Company at a price per New Share of NOK 5.31681.

Subject to timely payment of the aggregate exercise amount for the new shares on 19 June 2025, the Company expects that the share capital increase pertaining to the issuance of the new shares will be registered with the Norwegian Register of Business Enterprises on or about 24 June 2025 and that shares will be delivered to the VPS accounts of the subscribers to whom they are allocated on or about 25 June 2025.

The shares received upon exercise of Warrants will be listed on Euronext Oslo Børs under ISIN NO0013355859 and ticker code "LIFE". The shares will be listed as soon as the new shares are fully paid, the share capital increase pertaining to the issuance of the new shares has been registered in the Norwegian Register of Business Enterprises, and the new shares have been issued in the VPS.

The shares received upon exercise of Warrants may not be transferred or traded before (i) the new shares have been fully paid, (ii) the share capital increase pertaining to the issuance of the new shares has been registered with the Norwegian Register of Business Enterprises, and (iii) the new shares have been issued in the VPS.

data.title: Oil Eases as Traders Monitor Middle East Tensions — Market Talk
data.description: Oil prices slide after jumping more than 4% in the previous trading session as investors monitor tensions in the Middle East and the latest U.S. trade policy developments. Brent crude is down 1.1% to $68.99 a barrel, while WTI slips 1% to $67.46 a barrel. Both benchmarks surged on Wednesday after the U.S. ordered nonessential personnel out of parts of the Middle East amid heightened security concerns. The move comes ahead of the Iran nuclear talks set for this weekend in Oman. Traders are also closely monitoring U.S. trade policy following talks with China and reports that Trump plans to send letters to trading partners in the next one to two weeks setting unilateral tariff rates. Meanwhile, U.S. crude-oil inventories fell more than expected last week, though gasoline and distillate fuel stocks climbed. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0403 ET - Oil prices slide after jumping more than 4% in the previous trading session as investors monitor tensions in the Middle East and the latest U.S. trade policy developments. Brent crude is down 1.1% to $68.99 a barrel, while WTI slips 1% to $67.46 a barrel. Both benchmarks surged on Wednesday after the U.S. ordered nonessential personnel out of parts of the Middle East amid heightened security concerns. The move comes ahead of the Iran nuclear talks set for this weekend in Oman. Traders are also closely monitoring U.S. trade policy following talks with China and reports that Trump plans to send letters to trading partners in the next one to two weeks setting unilateral tariff rates. Meanwhile, U.S. crude-oil inventories fell more than expected last week, though gasoline and distillate fuel stocks climbed. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0350 ET - The immediate question arising from the China-U.S. London agreement is how much China will really relax its export controls, says Arthur Kroeber of Gavekal Research. "The best bet is that it will reopen rare-earth exports to commercial buyers enough for them to satisfy their ongoing need but not so much that they can stockpile," he writes. Beijing may be even stingier with buyers that feed into the U.S. defense supply chain. The U.S. and European auto and defense supply chains are deeply vulnerable to China's carefully-developed stranglehold on rare earths. Undoing that will take many years, Kroeber says, noting that Japan still relies on China for over half of its needs despite trying to reduce dependence for over a decade. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0349 ET - Gold futures rise on safe-haven demand. Futures are up 0.6% at $3,364.20 a troy ounce, having risen as high as $3,398.40/oz earlier in the session. The precious metal has gained after President Trump's negative commentary on negotiations with Iran over a nuclear agreement, Pepperstone's Ahmad Assiri says in a note. U.S. dollar weakness and high tariffs creating market uncertainty despite a tentative U.S.-China trade deal have also contributed to gold's gains. The U.S. delegation to Iranian negotiations is set to abstain from an intended meeting Sunday and the simultaneous evacuation of non-essential U.S. embassy personnel in Iraqi posts has reinforced perceptions of elevated tension, Assiri writes. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0340 ET - U.K. growth is pointing downward, ING's James Smith says. Data earlier showed the economy contracted more than expected at the start of the second quarter. April's 0.3% contraction points to more muted growth rates for the remainder of the year, particularly after this week's weak jobs data, Smith says in a note. Global uncertainty is exacerbating Britain's underyling problems and risks surrounding the U.K. growth outlook are building, he says.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0332 ET - The unwinding of tariff front-running after the imposition of U.S. levies widened the U.K.'s trade deficit in April, Pantheon Macroeconomics economists say in a note. Goods exports to the U.S. fell 2.0 billion pounds on the month in April, with sales to the European Union dropping too. "Exports should begin to stabilize in May now that the front-running has unwound and after President Trump began walking back some of his more ruinous tariffs," the economists say. However, the U.K.-U.S. trade agreement in May has yet to fully come into force so there could be weakness ahead, they say. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0330 ET - China-U.S. talks in London probably leave everything back to where it was a month ago, says Arthur Kroeber of Gavekal Research. As far as Gavekal can tell, the London agreement annuls the moves that lead to the breakdown of the prior truce but leaves in place the tariff structure. That implies an effective average tariff on China of 30% to 50%--enough to cause pain, but not much, since transshipment channels via third countries remain wide open, Kroeber writes. To sign a deal, China's two big asks will be a rollback of U.S. tech export controls, and more space for Chinese investment in the U.S. Both would require Trump to overcome resistance within Congress and the defense bureaucracy, Kroeber says, suggesting that the deal space remains narrow. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0328 ET - Sterling falls to a one-month low against the euro after data showed the U.K. economy contracted by 0.3% in April, more than economists had expected. The economy was partly hit by U.S. reciprocal tariffs, says XTB's Kathleen Brooks in a note. However, the pound's declines could be temporary as the U.K. has fewer exports to the U.S. than many other countries and has sealed a trade deal, she says. "Considering how seismic U.S. economic policy has been for the global economy, the U.K. may have got off lightly." The euro rises 0.2% to a high of 0.8499 pounds, according to LSEG data. Sterling trades steady against the dollar at $1.3545, falling from an earlier one-week high of $1.3593. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0323 ET - Companies are delaying, but not cancelling, dealmaking decisions since President Trump unveiled his plans to impose sweeping tariffs at the beginning of April, Deutsche Bank CEO Christian Sewing says at a Goldman Sachs conference. Some deals the German bank is working on won't be accounted for in the second quarter, but slip into the third, Sewing says. Overall, the performance of Deutsche Bank's investment bank in the second quarter will be in line with the year-earlier period, Sewing says. A weaker-than-expected performance in its origination-and-advisory activities will be offset by its fixed income-and-currencies and its financing operations, he says. Deutsche Bank shares fall 1.1%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0311 ET - The dollar falls to seven-week lows against a basket of currencies and against the euro, extending the previous day's drop after lower-than-forecast U.S. inflation data. The figures increase prospects of the U.S. Federal Reserve cutting interest rates. U.S. money markets fully price in a rate cut in October, with a strong chance of a reduction in September. They price another rate cut in December and more in 2026, LSEG data show. The dollar isn't benefiting from U.S.-China trade talks or from strong demand at Wednesday's 10-year Treasury auction, ING's Chris Turner says in a note. The DXY dollar index falls to a low of 98.247, while the euro hits a high of $1.1532, according to LSEG. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0248 ET - Employers' costs and tax rises are weighing on the U.K. economy, argues Hailey Low of think tank the National Institute of Economic and Social Research. Britain's GDP fall by 0.3% in April, with a drop in exports to the U.S. and wider tariff-led uncertainty ending the first quarter's energetic rise in activity, figures showed Thursday. But an increase in employers' tax contributions and wider cost pressures also look to be taking their toll, Low says. "It is more important than ever that policy remains focused onlaying the bedrock such that the U.K. is able to raise long-run GDP growth," she says.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0241 ET - Trade uncertainty is here to stay for the U.K., Deutsche Bank's Sanjay Raja tells investors in a note after the economy shrank by more than expected in April, the month in which Donald Trump set out his tariff blitz. The 0.3% monthly drop in GDP was driven by a downturn in factory output and in exports, pointing to the effects of the chillier trade backdrop, according to figures set out Thursday. And those headwinds won't fully ease in following months, Raja warns. Trade turmoil looks lasting, and the U.K. must also reckon with a weakening jobs market and restrictive monetary policy, he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

2139 ET - Any rollback of non-tariff barriers between China and the U.S. will open a window in which U.S. firms will try to amass rare earths and Chinese firms will stockpile chips, Capital Economics says. "But neither side will bank on that window remaining open," says Chief Asia Economist Mark Williams. In the past month, the U.S. put new controls on China-bound exports of chip-design software, chemicals, and parts for jet engines and nuclear plants. Even if that's walked back in exchange for access to rare earths, Chinese firms face tighter restrictions and much higher tariffs, he says. Both sides will look for alternative suppliers long term, especially as the U.S.-China relationship hangs on Trump's judgment. "A breakdown in the relationship is only a Truth Social post away." (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0803 GMT - Oil prices slide after jumping more than 4% in the previous trading session as investors monitor tensions in the Middle East and the latest U.S. trade policy developments. Brent crude is down 1.1% to $68.99 a barrel, while WTI slips 1% to $67.46 a barrel. Both benchmarks surged on Wednesday after the U.S. ordered nonessential personnel out of parts of the Middle East amid heightened security concerns. The move comes ahead of the Iran nuclear talks set for this weekend in Oman. Traders are also closely monitoring U.S. trade policy following talks with China and reports that Trump plans to send letters to trading partners in the next one to two weeks setting unilateral tariff rates. Meanwhile, U.S. crude-oil inventories fell more than expected last week, though gasoline and distillate fuel stocks climbed. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0800 GMT - BP could announce a flurry of disposals in the second half of the year, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. They write this after hosting a virtual call with BP's Chief Executive Murray Auchincloss. In the near term, the focus is on disposing infrastructure assets and some deals could be announced relatively soon, they say. Work on the Gelsenkirken refinery disposal continues while the Lightsource process is due to launch imminently, they add. Overall, BP is confident it can reach its $20 billion disposal target, the analysts write. Shares trade up 1.7% at 380 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0730 GMT - European oil stocks led the gains in initial trade in Europe. The stocks were boosted by yesterday's jump in oil prices, which left Brent crude trading around $69.24 a barrel Thursday morning. Geopolitical fears in the Middle East propelled yesterday's rise. In London, BP trades up 1.7% while Shell is up 1.26%. France's TotalEnergies rises 1.9%, Italy's Eni 1.6% and Spain's Repsol 1.35%. Portugal's Galp is up 1.46% and Norway's Equinor climbs 1.7%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0638 GMT - Medco Energi Internasional may benefit from new production by two oil-and-gas fields in the South Natuna Sea Block B at Indonesia's Riau Islands starting in 2Q, Nomura's Ahmad Maghfur Usman says in a note. Another tailwind is the newly signed multiparty gas swap agreement, which is expected to improve its overall sales by optimizing asset allocation, the analyst says. An earnings improvement at associate Amman Mineral Internasional following a smelter's ramp-up is a third tailwind. However, the Indonesian oil-and-gas company's 1Q earnings missed Nomura's expectations. The brokerage lowers the stock's target price to IDR1,700.00 from IDR2,000.00, with an unchanged buy rating. Verdhana Sekuritas analysts contributed to Nomura's report. Shares are 1.2% higher at IDR1,295.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0620 GMT - A geopolitical risk premium hasn't yet been priced into oil markets, says the ANZ Research team in a note. Markets have largely brushed off such risks, such as tensions rising in the Middle East between Iran and Israel, and the U.S.'s partial evacuation of its embassy in Iraq, they say. These events, however, significantly raise the risks of supply disruptions. ANZ is also watching the critical minerals industry, which is central to energy systems. The U.S.-China trade war continues to pose challenges to the U.S. energy sector, they add. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are down 0.6% at $67.76/bbl; Brent crude oil futures are 0.6% lower at $69.32/bbl. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0543 GMT - The crude oil market is set to be in surplus by 4Q, which may extend into 2026, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a research note. Brent may fall to mid-$50s per barrel by 1H 2026. Elevated OPEC spare capacity after production cuts suggests that it retains ample ability to quickly ramp up production, which has acted as a drag on prices, they say. This recent buildup in spare capacity has dampened crude oil's traditional response as a geopolitical hedge. "Amid greater-than-ample supply, the market has focused on signals tracking real-time growth expectations for the U.S., the world's largest consumer of oil," they say. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are down 0.5% at $67.82/bbl; Brent crude oil futures are 0.6% lower at $69.37/bbl.(sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0537 GMT - Crude oil prices have room to grow further given the backdrop of the U.S.-China tariff negotiations alongside easing U.S. inflation, Phillip Nova analysts write in a note. In the U.S., recent EIA data point to a larger-than-expected draw in inventories, reflecting healthy domestic demand, the analyst says. It appears that all fundamentals are tuned in tandem to boost oil prices, not only by Trump's unpredictability but the sensitivity of markets to news headlines, they say. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are down 0.3% at $67.96/bbl, while Brent crude oil futures are also 0.4% lower at $69.49/bbl.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0530 GMT - Oil prices are being pressured higher by fresh tensions in the Middle East. The U.S. is moving to draw down its presence in parts of the region to essential personnel as tensions rise ahead of the latest round of nuclear talks between Iran and the U.S. Meanwhile, the U.K. Maritime Trade Operations said in an advisory that "tensions within the region could lead to an escalation of military activity having a direct impact on mariners." That sent Brent briefly past $70/barrel, and put WTI near that level, but gains have since retraced as markets remain cautious amid trade war concerns, Maybank analysts say. "Prices are nearing key resistance levels, and momentum indicators are approaching overbought conditions," they said, staying wary of further oil price upside.(fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

data.title: Prudential Buys Back 300,000 Shares in London
data.description:
Prudential repurchased 300,000 shares on the London bourse at an average price of 8.97 pounds sterling apiece on Wednesday, according to a Thursday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

The insurer intends to cancel the repurchased shares.

Following the buyback, Pru will have 2,593,417,452 shares in issue.

data.title: Oil Prices to Stay Volatile Amid Geopolitical Tensions, Economic Backdrop — Market Talk
data.description: Oil prices are likely to stay volatile, according to Ahmad Assiri, research strategist at Pepperstone in a commentary. Markets are focused on geopolitical tensions and the global economic backdrop, the strategist says. "The interplay between geopolitical manoeuvring and underlying demand strength suggests that oil market dynamics will remain prone to swings," he says. Traders should factor in the likelihood of volatility tied to shifts in regional risk, he adds. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are 1.1% lower at $67.40/bbl; front-month Brent crude futures are 1.1% lower at $68.98/bbl. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)
